name: Tafsir al-Mizan
author: Allamah Muhammad Husayn Tabatabai
text:
# Ayahs

```arabic
۞ هُوَ ٱلَّذِى خَلَقَكُم مِّن نَّفْسٍ وَٰحِدَةٍ وَجَعَلَ مِنْهَا زَوْجَهَا لِيَسْكُنَ إِلَيْهَا ۖ فَلَمَّا تَغَشَّىٰهَا حَمَلَتْ حَمْلًا خَفِيفًا فَمَرَّتْ بِهِۦ ۖ فَلَمَّآ أَثْقَلَت دَّعَوَا ٱللَّهَ رَبَّهُمَا لَئِنْ ءَاتَيْتَنَا صَـٰلِحًا لَّنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ ٱلشَّـٰكِرِينَ (١٨٩)
```

```english
It is He who created you from a single soul, and made from it its mate, that he might find comfort with her. So when he had covered her, she bore a light burden and passed [some time] with it. When she had grown heavy, they both invoked Allah, their Lord: ‘If You give us a healthy [child], we will be surely grateful.’ (189)
```

```arabic
فَلَمَّآ ءَاتَىٰهُمَا صَـٰلِحًا جَعَلَا لَهُۥ شُرَكَآءَ فِيمَآ ءَاتَىٰهُمَا ۚ فَتَعَـٰلَى ٱللَّهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ (١٩٠)
```

```english
Then when He gave them a healthy [child], they ascribed partners to Him in what He had given them. Exalted is Allah above [having] any partners that they ascribe [to Him]! (190)
```

```arabic
أَيُشْرِكُونَ مَا لَا يَخْلُقُ شَيْـًٔا وَهُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ (١٩١)
```

```english
Do they ascribe [to Him] partners that create nothing and have been created themselves, (191)
```

```arabic
وَلَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ لَهُمْ نَصْرًا وَلَآ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَنصُرُونَ (١٩٢)
```

```english
and can neither help them, nor help themselves? (192)
```

```arabic
وَإِن تَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَى ٱلْهُدَىٰ لَا يَتَّبِعُوكُمْ ۚ سَوَآءٌ عَلَيْكُمْ أَدَعَوْتُمُوهُمْ أَمْ أَنتُمْ صَـٰمِتُونَ (١٩٣)
```

```english
If you call them to guidance, they will not follow you: it is the same to you whether you call them or whether you are silent. (193)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ ٱلَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِ ٱللَّهِ عِبَادٌ أَمْثَالُكُمْ ۖ فَٱدْعُوهُمْ فَلْيَسْتَجِيبُوا۟ لَكُمْ إِن كُنتُمْ صَـٰدِقِينَ (١٩٤)
```

```english
Indeed those whom you invoke besides Allah are creatures like you. So invoke them: they should answer you, if you are truthful. (194)
```

```arabic
أَلَهُمْ أَرْجُلٌ يَمْشُونَ بِهَآ ۖ أَمْ لَهُمْ أَيْدٍ يَبْطِشُونَ بِهَآ ۖ أَمْ لَهُمْ أَعْيُنٌ يُبْصِرُونَ بِهَآ ۖ أَمْ لَهُمْ ءَاذَانٌ يَسْمَعُونَ بِهَا ۗ قُلِ ٱدْعُوا۟ شُرَكَآءَكُمْ ثُمَّ كِيدُونِ فَلَا تُنظِرُونِ (١٩٥)
```

```english
Do they have any feet to walk with? Do they have any hands to grasp with? Do they have any eyes to see with? Do they have any ears to hear with? Say, ‘Invoke your partners [that you ascribe to Allah] and try out your stratagems against me without granting me any respite. (195)
```

```arabic
إِنَّ وَلِـِّۧىَ ٱللَّهُ ٱلَّذِى نَزَّلَ ٱلْكِتَـٰبَ ۖ وَهُوَ يَتَوَلَّى ٱلصَّـٰلِحِينَ (١٩٦)
```

```english
My guardian is indeed Allah who sent down the Book, and He takes care of the righteous. (196)
```

```arabic
وَٱلَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِن دُونِهِۦ لَا يَسْتَطِيعُونَ نَصْرَكُمْ وَلَآ أَنفُسَهُمْ يَنصُرُونَ (١٩٧)
```

```english
Those whom you invoke besides Him can neither help you, nor help themselves. (197)
```

```arabic
وَإِن تَدْعُوهُمْ إِلَى ٱلْهُدَىٰ لَا يَسْمَعُوا۟ ۖ وَتَرَىٰهُمْ يَنظُرُونَ إِلَيْكَ وَهُمْ لَا يُبْصِرُونَ (١٩٨)
```

```english
If you call them to guidance, they will not hear. You see them facing you, but they do not see.’ (198)
```

## (Commentary)

The discourse in these verses follows the same pattern as the other verses of this Surah regarding the covenants of the human species and their frequent violation.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ نَفْسٍ واحِدَةٍ** "It is He who created you from a single soul" until the end of the two verses. The discourse in these two verses proceeds as a parable struck for the children of Adam regarding their violation of the covenant they had pledged, and their wrongdoing against the signs of Allah.

The meaning is: **هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ** "It is He who created you," O assembly of the children of Adam, **مِنْ نَفْسٍ واحِدَةٍ** "from a single soul," who is your father, **وَجَعَلَ مِنْها** "and made from it"—meaning from its kind—**زَوْجَها لِيَسْكُنَ** "its mate, so that he might find rest"—referring to the man who is the single soul—**إِلَيْها** "in her"—meaning in the mate who is his wife. **فَلَمَّا تَغَشَّاها** "Then when he covered her"—and "covering" (التغشي) refers to sexual intercourse—**حَمَلَتْ حَمْلاً خَفِيفاً** "she carried a light burden," and the thing carried is the sperm-drop (النطفة), which is light. **فَمَرَّتْ بِهِ** "and she went about with it"—meaning the wife continued with her pregnancy, coming and going, standing and sitting, until the sperm-drop grew in her womb and became a heavy fetus that burdened the wife. **فَلَمَّا أَثْقَلَتْ دَعَوَا اللهَ رَبَّهُما** "Then when she became heavy, they both invoked Allah, their Lord," and they made a pact with Him and gave Him their word: **لَئِنْ آتَيْتَنا** "If You give us"—and provide us with—**صالِحاً** "a righteous child"—one fit for life and survival by being a sound human being, complete in limbs and free from deformity or defect, for that is what is hoped for a child at birth and the start of its development, rather than religious righteousness—**لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الشَّاكِرِينَ** "we will surely be among the grateful" to You by manifesting Your blessing and devoting ourselves to You regarding its affair, not inclining toward any cause besides You, nor attaching ourselves to anything other than You.

**فَلَمَّا آتاهُما صالِحاً** "But when He gave them a righteous child," as they had asked, and made it a sound human being fit for survival, and their eyes were delighted by it, **جَعَلا لَهُ شُرَكاءَ فِيما آتاهُما** "they set up partners for Him in what He had given them" of the righteous child, as love and compassion for the child prompted them to attach themselves to every cause besides Him and to submit to everything other than Him, despite having stipulated for Him that they would be grateful to Him and not ungrateful for His blessing and Lordship (الربوبية). Thus, they violated their covenant and their condition.

Such is the state of humanity in general, except for those upon whom Allah has mercy; they are preoccupied with breaking their covenants, failing their promises, and not fulfilling their pledge with Allah. **فَتَعالَى اللهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ** "Exalted is Allah above what they polytheize (يشركون)."

The story—as you see—can be intended to explain the state of the two parents of the human species in their procreation of children in a general, species-oriented sense. For every human being is the offspring of two parents, and human multiplicity is the result of two parents giving birth to a child, as in His saying, the Exalted: **يا أَيُّهَا النَّاسُ إِنَّا خَلَقْناكُمْ مِنْ ذَكَرٍ وَأُنْثى وَجَعَلْناكُمْ شُعُوباً وَقَبائِلَ** "O mankind! Indeed We created you from a male and a female, and made you nations and tribes" (49:13).

The prevailing state of parents, while they love their child and feel compassion for it, is that they naturally devote themselves to Allah regarding the affair of their child, even if they do not notice the details of their devotion—just as a sea traveler devotes himself to Allah, the Glorified, when the waves clash and begin to play with him; he turns exclusively to his Lord even if he had never worshipped a lord before. This is simply a state of the heart (حال قلبي) that a human being is compelled toward.

Thus, the parents have a devotion to their Lord regarding the affair of their child: "If You give us a righteous child that we are pleased with, we will surely be among the grateful." But when He responds to them and gives them a righteous child, they set up partners for Him and cling to every cause for its protection and upbringing, seeking refuge in every shelter.

This view is supported by His saying at the end of the verse: **فَتَعالَى اللهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ** "Exalted is Allah above what they polytheize." For if the "soul" and its "mate" at the beginning of the discourse referred to two specific human individuals, such as Adam and Eve for instance, the phrasing should have been: "Exalted is Allah above their (dual) polytheism" or "above what they (dual) associated."

Furthermore, the Exalted follows this verse with other verses censuring polytheism (الشرك) and rebuking the polytheists (المشركين) in a manner whose apparent meaning is polytheism in the sense of worshipping other than Allah. Far be it from the chosen one of Allah, Adam, to worship other than Allah, when Allah, the Glorified, has explicitly stated that He chose him and guided him, and has stated that there is no way for misguidance (الضلال) to reach those whom Allah has guided. And what misguidance is more astray than worshipping other than Allah? The Exalted said: **ثُمَّ اجْتَباهُ رَبُّهُ فَتابَ عَلَيْهِ وَهَدى** "Then his Lord chose him, and He turned to him clemently, and guided him" (20:122), and He said: **وَمَنْ يَهْدِ اللهُ فَهُوَ الْمُهْتَدِ** "Whomever Allah guides, he is the rightly guided" (17:97), and He said: **وَمَنْ أَضَلُّ مِمَّنْ يَدْعُوا مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ مَنْ لا يَسْتَجِيبُ لَهُ إِلى يَوْمِ الْقِيامَةِ** "Who is more astray than him who invokes besides Allah such as would not respond to him until the Day of Resurrection?" (46:5). By this, it becomes clear that misguidance and polytheism are not attributed to Adam, even if we do not affirm his prophethood, or if we affirm it but do not affirm the infallibility (العصمة) of the prophets (peace be upon them).

If the "soul" and its "mate" in the story were intended to be Adam and his wife, then their "polytheism" mentioned in the verse would mean that they became occupied with the upbringing of the child and concerned themselves with its affair by managing causes and factors, and this diverted them from some of the orientation they had toward their Lord and their sincerity (الخلوص) in that. Evidence for this is His saying, the Exalted, narrating from them: **لَنَكُونَنَّ مِنَ الشَّاكِرِينَ** "we will surely be among the grateful." It has already been mentioned in the commentary on the beginning of this Surah, regarding His saying: **وَلا تَجِدُ أَكْثَرَهُمْ شاكِرِينَ** "and You will not find most of them grateful" (7:17), that the "grateful" (الشاكرين) in Quranic terminology are the sincere ones (المخلصون) — *those purified by Allah* — over whom Iblis has no way and into whose hearts heedlessness (الغفلة) does not creep. Thus, the reproach directed at them in His saying: **فَتَعالَى اللهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ** "Exalted is Allah above what they polytheize" would only be for polytheism in the sense of being occupied with other than Allah through cosmic causes, which constitutes a turning away from the heart's sincerity toward the Exalted.

However, there remains the issue of His saying: **فَتَعالَى اللهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ** "Exalted is Allah above what they polytheize" being in the plural form, and its being followed by what apparently refers to polytheism in the sense of worshipping other than Allah.

Some have attempted to resolve this by suggesting that the verse involves specification (التخصيص) first and generalization (التعميم) second, the opposite of His saying, the Exalted: **هُوَ الَّذِي يُسَيِّرُكُمْ فِي الْبَرِّ وَالْبَحْرِ حَتَّى إِذا كُنْتُمْ فِي الْفُلْكِ وَجَرَيْنَ بِهِمْ بِرِيحٍ طَيِّبَةٍ** "It is He who carries you across land and sea. When you are in the ships, and they sail with them with a favorable wind..." (10:22), where He first addressed the general population regarding travel, then specified the discourse to those among them who sail in ships. The verse we are discussing specifies the beginning of the story to Adam and his wife—they being the ones intended by His saying: **هُوَ الَّذِي خَلَقَكُمْ مِنْ نَفْسٍ واحِدَةٍ وَجَعَلَ مِنْها زَوْجَها** "It is He who created you from a single soul and made from it its mate"—then the account of Adam and his wife ends, and the polytheists among the children of Adam are specifically mentioned, those who asked what they asked and then set up partners for Him in what He gave them. That is, for every pair of them who give birth to a child, this is their state of making a covenant and then violating it.

The critique of this is that His saying: **هُوَ الَّذِي يُسَيِّرُكُمْ** "It is He who carries you..." is surrounded by definitive contextual clues (قرينة قطعية) that indicate the intent and remove ambiguity, unlike the progression from the specific to the general in this verse, which causes ambiguity and is not resorted to in eloquent speech (الكلام البليغ)—unless His saying: **فَتَعالَى اللهُ عَمَّا يُشْرِكُونَ** "Exalted is Allah above what they polytheize" until the end of the verses is made a contextual clue for that.

In any case, this view is like a combination of the first two views, by interpreting the beginning of the verse according to the second view and its end according to the first.

Sometimes the previous objection is countered by suggesting there is an omission and connection (حذف وإيصال) in the speech, the estimation being: "But when He gave them—meaning Adam and Eve—a righteous child, their children set up partners for Him." Thus, the possessed noun (المضاف), which is "children," was omitted, and the possessor (المضاف إليه), which is the dual pronoun indicated in His saying: "they (dual) set up" (جعلا), was put in its place. The critique of this is that there is no evidence for it.

Some exegetes have accepted the difficulty and conceded that the two intended are Adam and his wife, and that they associated partners with Allah, acting upon narrations (روايات) that have been transmitted regarding the story from some sources. These are fabricated (موضوعة) or interpolated (مدسوسة) reports that contradict the Book, and there is no way to accept the likes of them.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **أَيُشْرِكُونَ ما لا يَخْلُقُ شَيْئاً وَهُمْ يُخْلَقُونَ** "Do they ascribe as partners [to Him] those who do not create anything and are themselves created?" until the end of the three verses. Although the beginning of the verses could possibly refer to polytheism with idols or with other causes besides Allah—reliance upon which is a type of polytheism—their conclusion clearly shows that the intent is polytheism with idols taken as gods, which are inanimate objects (جماد) that cannot help those who worship them nor help themselves, and are not aware of any invocation or its absence.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **إِنَّ الَّذِينَ تَدْعُونَ مِنْ دُونِ اللهِ عِبادٌ أَمْثالُكُمْ** "Indeed those whom you invoke besides Allah are servants like yourselves" until His saying **يَسْمَعُونَ بِها** "they hear with." This is an argument (احتجاج) for the content of the three preceding verses. The meaning is: We only said they are created and have no power over anything because they are servants (عباد) like yourselves; just as you are created and governed, so are they.

The proof for this is that they do not respond to you if you invoke them, so invoke them if you are truthful in your claim that they possess knowledge and power—and the claim of their possessing knowledge and power was attributed to them because your invoking them indicates that. And how could they respond to you? The legs and hands you have assigned to them do not walk or grasp, and the eyes and ears you have fashioned for them do not see or hear, because they are inanimate objects.

In these verses, the term "servants" (العباد) is applied to inanimate objects.

**His saying, the Exalted:** **قُلِ ادْعُوا شُرَكاءَكُمْ ثُمَّ كِيدُونِ فَلا تُنْظِرُونِ** "Say, ‘Invoke your partners, then conspire against me and give me no respite’" until the end of the verses. Then He commanded him (peace be upon him and his progeny) to turn upon them despite their seeking victory through their lords and gods, by way of challenge (التحدي) and miracle (الإعجاز), so that his way might be distinguished from theirs, and it might become clear that his Lord is Allah, to Whom belongs all knowledge and power, and that their lords possess no knowledge to guide them to anything, nor power to help them in anything.

So He said: Say to them, invoke your partners to help you against me, then conspire against me and do not give me respite or delay. Indeed, my Lord helps me and repels your conspiracy from me, for He is the One who sent down the Book to guide people by it, and He takes charge of the righteous (الصالحين) among His servants and helps them. He is the one who says: "Indeed the earth shall be inherited by My righteous servants," and I am among the righteous, so He will inevitably help me. As for your lords whom you invoke besides Him, they cannot help you nor help themselves, and they neither hear nor see; they have neither power nor knowledge.

In these verses, the Prophet (peace be upon him and his progeny) is commanded to inform them that he is among the righteous (الصالحين), and nothing similar to this has been recorded in what the Quran reports of the righteousness of the prophets regarding anyone other than him (peace be upon him and his progeny).

In them is a challenge (التحدي) against the idols and their worshippers, just as other prophets (peace be upon them) challenged them.

## (Narrative Discussion)

In *al-Uyun*, with its chain of transmission from Abu al-Salt al-Harawi, from Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) in a tradition: al-Ma'mun said to him: "Then what is the meaning of His saying, the Exalted: **فَلَمَّا آتاهُما صالِحاً جَعَلا لَهُ شُرَكاءَ فِيما آتاهُما** 'But when He gave them a righteous child, they set up partners for Him in what He had given them'?" Imam al-Rida (peace be upon him) said: **إن حواء ولدت لآدم خمس مائة بطن ـ في كل بطن ذكرا وأنثى ، وإن آدم وحواء عاهدا الله تعالى ودعواه وقالا : لئن آتيتنا صالحا لنكونن من الشاكرين ، فلما آتاهما صالحا من النسل خلقا سويا ـ بريئا من الزمانة والعاهة ـ كان ما آتاهما صنفين : صنفا ذكرانا وصنفا إناثا ـ فجعل الصنفان لله تعالى ذكره شركاء فيما آتاهما ، ولم يشكراه كشكر أبويهما له عز وجل** "Indeed, Eve bore for Adam five hundred births—in each birth a male and a female. Adam and Eve made a covenant with Allah the Exalted and invoked Him, saying: 'If You give us a righteous child, we will surely be among the grateful.' When He gave them righteous offspring—created soundly, free from chronic illness and deformity—what He gave them were two types: a type of males and a type of females. So the two types set up partners for Allah, exalted be His mention, in what He had given them, and they did not thank Him as their parents had thanked Him, Mighty and Majestic is He." al-Ma'mun said: "I bear witness that you are truly the son of the Messenger of Allah."

**I say:** Its return is to some of the previous views in repelling what was objected against the verse. Several narrations have been reported regarding the interpretation of the verse, narrated from Samurah ibn Jundub, Ubayy, Zayd, and Ibn Abbas, stating that Adam and Eve's children would not survive, so the Satan commanded them—or commanded Eve—to name him Abd al-Harith so that he might live—and al-Harith was his name in heaven. In some of them: Abd al-Shams. In some of them: that he frightened her that she would give birth to a camel, a cow, or some other beast, and he stipulated for her that if she named him Abd al-Harith, she would give birth to a sound human being. These traditions are fabricated (موضوعة) or interpolated (مدسوسة) from the *Isra'iliyyat* (Judeo-Christian traditions).

It has been narrated in *al-Majma'*, from the *Tafsir* of al-Ayyashi, from them (peace be upon them): "Their polytheism was a polytheism of obedience (طاعة) and was not a polytheism of disobedience (معصية)." Its apparent meaning is that it follows the course of those traditions, so its status is the same as theirs. And how can one distinguish between obedience and worship, especially in the case of Iblis, when the Exalted has said: **أَلَمْ أَعْهَدْ إِلَيْكُمْ يا بَنِي آدَمَ أَنْ لا تَعْبُدُوا الشَّيْطانَ إِنَّهُ لَكُمْ عَدُوٌّ مُبِينٌ وَأَنِ اعْبُدُونِي** "Did I not exhort you, O children of Adam, saying, ‘Do not worship Satan. He is indeed your manifest enemy. Worship Me’" (36:61)? Despite this, some have mentioned that these narrations do not indicate more than polytheism in naming, and that is neither disbelief (كفر) nor disobedience (معصية); al-Tabari chose this view.
